speaker: interviewer
text: And we ended up where you were interning in New York and how that went.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, New York was not a particularly good hospital, really. And when I transferred to Wilmington, I was much more satisfied. And I did a rotating internship, which means a couple of months in every department. And people were reasonably nice. And I think I did quite well. I-- at the end of my-- I had-- during my internship, I became pregnant with my son. And when I finished my internship, I was in my seventh month, I believe. And I-- I delivered in-- I finished my internship in November, and I delivered in February. And my son, Peter, was born. I stayed at home for almost a year. And then I started to take a general practice residency in Wilmington because I couldn't get into a pediatric residency, which I originally wanted. And after about 10 months, I was pregnant again. And my daughter was born in April of 1960. And I had stayed home for a year, for my whole pregnancy. And I stayed home after she was born, also. And we moved to Phoenix in January of '61. And after coming to Phoenix, I had made-- my husband got a job with Motorola. And I had made inquiries so that I could do a pediatric residency in Phoenix. And it was possible, so after we came to Phoenix, I started my pediatric residency at the county hospital here in January-- in February of 1961. And I did-- I finished my residency in '63. And I opened a private practice for a couple of years, but it was really too difficult with the children. And I decided not to do it, mainly after we had a bad accident. And my daughter was really critically injured at that time. And that sort of really gave me a shock. And that induced me to close my practice and go for a nine-to-five job, which I did. And I worked for the County Health Department for about 20 years. And after that, I worked for ASU for five years as a physician, you know, in-- in their health services. And after that, I have been with Cigna now for about 10 years, lately only part time. And that sums up my--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my children's names are Peter and Judy. And Peter is a physician. He lives in Atlanta. And he just got married about six months ago and is expecting a baby, which I'm very happy about. And my daughter got a law degree, but she works for the IRS in their criminal investigation department. And she's, unfortunately, not married. [LAUGHS] Hopefully, it will happen one of these days. And that's about it. My husband is retired. He worked for Motorola for 25 years, and now he is retired. That sums it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about today's situation? And what is your feeling about this possibly ever happening again, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Holocaust? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I just hope it doesn't happen. That's-- that's all I can say. I don't think it could happen in the United States. I really don't think it could happen here. I think all sorts of persecutions could theoretically happen but not something like the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you became aware of what happened to your parents and your family? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Only really when we-- when we came to Israel. I didn't know anything while I was in Mauritius because nobody knew anything. All I knew is that my parents-- the last communication I had from my parents was in May of '32-- of '42. And at that time, they wrote to me that they are going to a camp. And I never heard from them again. And so I have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not had the guts to investigate what happened to them. I-- I really don't want to know, if you ask me. I know that in '42, the people who went to Terezin, which my parents did-- I found that out from somebody who saw them in Terezin. And I know that they left after a week and went-- from all I know, probably, to Majdanek. Those were the transports who went to Majdanek. And everybody knows what happened in Majdanek. And whether they went to Majdanek or some other place, I really-- I really don't care. I really don't want to know. I just-- I have not felt investigating the situation-- some people want to know exactly when, what, where, how. I don't want to know. It's hard enough without knowing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my brother? My brother came to Israel or Palestine in-- 1940 in 1939. He left home before I did. And he stayed-- my father has, or had three sisters in Israel. They're all dead by now. And my brother lived-- sort of alternated between two of them. And he was 14 years old when he got there. And he went to school for a couple of years. And he took some training in-- I still-- let's say welding. It's not welding. It's something else. I can't remember the name. I know the name in Hebrew. He was a [SPEAKING HEBREW]. And he worked for the railway for a couple of years. And-- and then he went to work for some private businessmen and had a very good job, actually. He got married, and he had a child. My brother had always been slightly neurotic, even at home as a child, and somehow, in Israel, the situation got worse. I think he had a very hard time during the war because he was really the only-- staying with my relatives, he was not in a kibbutz with people like him. And he was the only one in this area who didn't have any parents, and he took it very hard. And my relatives are-- are not terribly sensitive people. And I think he had really hard time with them. And so he-- when I came back from Mauritius, he already was pretty unhappy. And we had long discussions. And he felt very bad about not having finished any really higher schooling. And he wasn't happy in his job. But he didn't have the guts to do something about it, and so he never did. But eventually, I think he was relatively satisfied with the job that he was having, until his boss died. And then his son-in-law took over. And I don't think he had a very good relationship with the son-in-law. And so things were getting worse. And somehow, he got into a depression, and he committed suicide. And it was terrible, really. He was a very bright, very talented, extremely good-looking guy. And it was very bad for his wife and his daughter. And for me, I had guilt feelings for a long time, what I could have done to help him. But I think I've stopped the guilt feelings. I think there was nothing to really do to help him. He refused to go to a doctor. It was one of those things. So that's the story of my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Ruth.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're on.

speaker: interviewee
text: We on?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like in the United States and since you're married to Ruth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we-- we came to the United States. And we were-- really, in comparison with other people-- immigrants or whatever-- I think we had a fairly easy time in comparison, since we came with two degrees, and there was demand for-- for our skills. And my dear wifey here-- she has been a trooper. She has been a big support.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel, Ruth, about Rudy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he's a good guy. He drives me nuts sometimes but we get along, and that's the main thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. How long have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what-- you figure out, it's going to be 42 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: 41, as of right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: 42 next-- in March.

speaker: interviewee
text: In March, it's going to be 42.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep, yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture? OK. This picture was taken either in 1938 or 1939. I don't remember exactly. From right to left on top, this is my mother, my grandmother-- my maternal grandmother, who lived with us, is in the middle on the-- The right side. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I did it the other way. It was from left to right. And the third picture on top is my father. The picture underneath is me. And the picture on the lower left is my brother. And the picture in the middle is of the four of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is picture of my home town, Jihlava, in Czechoslovakia. I don't know exactly when this picture was taken. But it very much looks like that still today. This is part of the town square with an old church and the city hall next to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a group of registered nurses and nurses aides in Mauritius, all from our transport. All of us worked in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would assume that this is probably-- before we got the uniforms, I would think in 1941 or 1942. I don't know exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is the building which housed the men. Each man had his own cell. And the picture on the left shows the inside of that building, with cell next to cell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: On Mauritius. I'm sorry. I thought that was obvious. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is a picture of the barracks, which comprised the women's camp. And on the left, there's the picture of the wall and the gate which divided the-- the men's camp and the women's camp, again on Mauritius. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the back are-- are the passes that we had to get in order to leave the camp. It was dated. And the time was put down, the time of our exit and the time of our return. And in the front are two items, which were produced by some of the inmates of our camp. They were actually two birthday presents for me. One is a ring out of tortoise shell. And the other one is a little wallet that was handmade by somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Atlantic Ocean.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, you're right. Of the ship

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right, there's a picture of the ship just to show the crowded conditions. On the-- on the right, it's the same ship during the time when they tried to strip the boat of all the wood in order to provide material for our departure. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'm sorry my children can't be here today. But this is a picture of both of them, Peter and Judy. I assume they know who Peter is and who Judy is.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the Steckl family, from left to right, Ruth, Peter, Judy, and Rudy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I would say about five years ago-- 1991, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Phoenix?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think it's in Phoenix. I don't remember. It might be Las Vegas. I think it-- well, we were for dinner, so we were celebrating something. Do you know where that picture is from that I have here?
